Kerberos

Kerberos is the primary authentication protocol used in Active Directory environments. It authenticates users and services using encrypted tickets instead of transmitting passwords over the network. Kerberos provides Single Sign-On (SSO), Mutual Authentication, and secure access to network resources through a trusted Key Distribution Center (KDC).

Objectives

  • Securely authenticate users and services.
  • Eliminate password transmission over the network.
  • Provide Single Sign-On (SSO).
  • Support mutual authentication.
  • Protect against replay attacks using tickets and session keys.

Core Components

Key Distribution Center (KDC)

A trusted service running on every Domain Controller that manages Kerberos authentication.

Authentication Server (AS)

  • Authenticates users.
  • Issues a Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT).

Ticket Granting Server (TGS)

  • Validates TGTs.
  • Issues Service Tickets (ST) for specific services.

Service Principal Name (SPN)

A unique identifier for a service instance. The KDC uses the SPN to determine which Service Ticket to issue.

Session Keys

Temporary encryption keys generated during authentication to secure communication between clients and services.

Authentication Flow

  1. Client requests authentication (AS-REQ).
  2. KDC authenticates the user and returns a TGT (AS-REP).
  3. Client requests access to a service (TGS-REQ).
  4. KDC returns a Service Ticket (TGS-REP).
  5. Client presents the ticket to the service (AP-REQ).
  6. Service validates the ticket and optionally performs mutual authentication (AP-REP).

Advantages

Passwords are never transmitted, Single Sign-On (SSO) is supported, mutual authentication is provided, tickets are protected with strong encryption and limited lifetimes, and the protocol is generally more secure than NTLM.

Common Attacks

  • Kerberoasting
  • AS-REP Roasting
  • Golden Ticket
  • Silver Ticket
  • Pass-the-Ticket (PtT)
  • Overpass-the-Hash
  • Delegation Abuse

Best Practices

  • Prefer Kerberos over NTLM.
  • Protect the KRBTGT account.
  • Use strong passwords or gMSAs for service accounts.
  • Keep system clocks synchronized.
  • Monitor Kerberos authentication events.
